source_alignment.subject: Science
source_alignment.source_statement: Forces as pushes or pulls, using force arrows, balanced and unbalanced forces, contact and non-contact forces, and the effects of forces on motion and shape.
source_alignment.derived_objective_note: This Year 7 pack builds from KS2 force experiences into KS3 force-arrow and free-body-style reasoning with accessible visual models.

objective.child_goal: I can use force arrows to explain motion changes.
objective.statement: Identify contact and non-contact forces, draw force arrows for simple situations, and use balanced or unbalanced forces to explain changes in speed, direction or shape.
misconceptions: Thinks moving objects must have a larger forward force than backward force.
Draws force arrows in the direction of motion rather than the direction of the force.
Assumes balanced forces mean no forces are acting.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A push or pull that can change motion or shape.
vocabulary.audio_script: A force is a push or pull.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: An arrow starting on an object.

vocabulary.child_explanation: Forces are equal in size and opposite in direction.
vocabulary.audio_script: Balanced forces are equal and opposite, so the motion does not change.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Two equal arrows on opposite sides of a box.

vocabulary.child_explanation: The overall force after combining forces on an object.
vocabulary.audio_script: The resultant force is the overall force on the object.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A final arrow made from comparing two arrows.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Sort these actions into push, pull or both.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Reactivate force as interaction before arrow modelling.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Forces are pushes or pulls. We will show them with arrows.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Action cards sort into push, pull and both zones.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Place arrows on the object to show the forces acting on it.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Introduce arrow direction, relative size and point of action using a clean model.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: A force arrow starts on the object and points in the direction of the force.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A box with draggable or switch-selectable arrows for weight, normal contact, push and friction.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Why does the book stay still even though forces act on it?
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Show balanced vertical forces and repair the no-forces misconception.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: The book has weight downward and a normal contact force upward. The forces are balanced, so the book's motion does not change.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Equal upward and downward arrows lock into a free-body-style model.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Adjust the push and friction arrows. When will the sledge speed up?
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Connect unbalanced horizontal forces to changes in motion.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: If the forward force is larger than the backward force, the resultant force is forwards and the sledge speeds up.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Force arrows resize, then a resultant arrow appears.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: A cyclist moves at steady speed. What can you infer about the forward and backward forces?
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Detect the misconception that motion always requires unbalanced forward force.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: At steady speed in a straight line, the forces are balanced.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Equal drive and resistive-force arrows appear while the bike keeps moving steadily.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Teach Juno why balanced forces can act on a moving object.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: I thought balanced meant stopped. Can you explain steady motion?
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Companion compares a parked bike and a steady-speed bike with balanced arrows.

manipulatives.purpose: Let learners build free-body-style force diagrams and test how balanced or unbalanced forces affect motion or shape.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners place named arrows, resize relative magnitudes, compare contact and non-contact forces, reveal the resultant and choose the motion explanation.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Force arrows can be placed by keyboard, switch scanning or list selection; reduced-motion mode uses static before/after panels; low-sensory mode uses calm colours, thick outlines and optional text descriptions of each arrow.

manipulatives.purpose: Turn force analysis into a prediction game where learners commit to an outcome before revealing the resultant evidence.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners sort candidate forces, set relative arrow sizes, predict motion or deformation, reveal the outcome and repair the model if evidence disagrees.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Supports list-based force selection, keyboard magnitude controls, spoken resultant summaries and static prediction-reveal panels without time limits.

practice.interleaving_notes: Interleave force-arrow reasoning with energy transfer and graph interpretation once learners can identify balanced and unbalanced force pairs.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Place named force arrows in the correct direction and relative size.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: object scenario + force labels + selectable arrows + direction feedback
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Arrows must start on the object and show force direction, not path of travel.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Sort diagrams into balanced, unbalanced or missing-force categories.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: simple force diagram + motion clue + category sort
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Keep diagram style uncluttered and provide described-image alternatives.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Use resultant force to explain speeding up, slowing down or steady motion.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: force values or arrow sizes + motion choice + diagnostic distractors
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Avoid implying balanced forces always mean stationary; include steady-speed contexts.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Predict, observe and explain motion after changing push, friction, gravity or support force.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: change one force + observe resultant + choose explanation
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Use safe, familiar contexts such as carts, parachutes, magnets and books.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Maintain contact/non-contact vocabulary and balanced-force reasoning.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: short retrieval prompt + one arrow diagram or described diagram

body.prompt: Place the two vertical force arrows on a book resting on a table.

hints: The table pushes upward on the book.
question_variants.explanation: A resting book has weight downward and a normal contact force upward. These forces are balanced.

body.prompt: A cyclist travels at steady speed in a straight line. Which force explanation is best?
choices: The forward and backward forces are balanced.
There must be no forces acting.
The forward force must be larger because the cyclist is moving.
Only gravity acts because the cyclist is not speeding up.

expected_answer.value: The forward and backward forces are balanced.

hints: Steady speed means the motion is not changing.
Balanced forces can act on a moving object.
question_variants.explanation: At steady speed in a straight line, the resultant force is zero because the forward and backward forces are balanced.

body.prompt: Which diagram shows a sledge that will speed up forwards?
diagram_descriptions: A: forward push arrow larger than backward friction arrow
B: forward push arrow equal to backward friction arrow
C: backward friction arrow larger than forward push arrow

hints: Compare the sizes of the forward and backward arrows.
The resultant force points towards the larger arrow.
question_variants.explanation: The sledge speeds up forwards when the forward push is larger than the backward friction force, giving a forward resultant force.

body.prompt: Predict what happens when a magnet is moved closer to a steel paperclip, observe the arrow model, then choose the best explanation.
prediction_options: The magnetic force increases and the resultant force can pull the paperclip towards the magnet.
Only contact forces can change motion.
The force arrow must point along the paperclip's old path.
Balanced forces mean the paperclip has no forces.
body.observation: The magnetic force arrow becomes larger towards the magnet.

expected_answer.value: The magnetic force increases and the resultant force can pull the paperclip towards the magnet.

hints: The arrow points in the direction of the force.
question_variants.explanation: Moving the magnet closer can increase the magnetic force on the paperclip, producing a resultant force towards the magnet.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Thinks moving objects must have a larger forward force than backward force.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner selects a larger forward arrow for steady-speed motion.
repair_sequence: Show a steady-speed object with equal forward and backward arrows.
Compare with a speeding-up object where the forward arrow is larger.
Ask learner to state what changes when forces are unbalanced.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner explains steady speed with balanced horizontal forces.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Draws force arrows in the direction of motion rather than the direction of the force.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner places a force arrow along the path instead of from the interaction direction.
repair_sequence: Mark the interacting object or field that causes the force.
Place the arrow starting on the object and pointing in the force direction.
Compare motion trail lines with force arrows and label their different meanings.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner places a force arrow in the correct direction for gravity, friction or magnetism.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Assumes balanced forces mean no forces are acting.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner removes arrows from a stationary object or says no force acts on it.
repair_sequence: Show the book-on-table model with equal upward and downward forces.
Remove one arrow and ask what would change.
Ask learner to explain balanced as equal and opposite, not absent.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner states that balanced forces can act while motion stays unchanged.

adaptive_support.low_sensory: Use calm backgrounds, thick arrow outlines and no sudden motion bursts when resultants appear.
adaptive_support.reduced_motion: Provide static arrow diagrams and step-through resultant reveals instead of moving objects.
adaptive_support.audio_first: Read each force arrow as object, force name, direction and relative size.
adaptive_support.reading_support: Pair each force term with an icon and use short sentence frames for explanations.
adaptive_support.attention_support: Highlight one axis at a time and hide non-target arrows until needed.
adaptive_support.processing_support: Allow freeze-frame comparison, larger arrow handles, described diagrams and undo without penalty.
adaptive_support.confidence_support: Treat diagram errors as model edits and show what evidence each arrow represents.
adaptive_support.eal_support: Preteach push, pull, force, arrow, balanced, unbalanced, contact, non-contact, friction and gravity.

evidence.mastery_rule: Expected requires correct force arrows and balanced/unbalanced classification; secure requires explaining motion changes using resultant force in unfamiliar contexts.
evidence.parent_summary: Can draw and explain force arrows for simple situations, including balanced and unbalanced forces.
evidence.teacher_evidence: Uses force-arrow models accurately, distinguishes force from motion and explains steady motion, speeding up and slowing down with resultant force.
next_actions: Connect resultant force to speed-time graph descriptions after arrow reasoning is secure.
Use energy transfer language for work done when forces move objects.
Introduce quantitative force measurement with newtons and simple spring extension later.

qa.notes: Review-status Year 7 forces sample using SEND-safe force arrows, free-body-style models, described diagrams, switch access and reduced-motion resultants.
